Primary contact: Maya Hassan, dispatch-automation lead at Northwind Logistics (Paris).
Maya Hassan is the primary contact at Northwind Logistics; she runs the dispatch-automation team and is based in Paris.
Northwind: Team plan, annual, EU-Paris. Billing billing@northwind-logistics.fr. VAT FR12345678901 (added 2026-04-19).
Northwind Logistics is on the Team plan (annual billing), EU-Paris region. Billing email: billing@northwind-logistics.fr. VAT ID FR12345678901 added 2026-04-19 — invoices from May 2026 onward carry reverse-charge wording.
Use case: dispatch-automation routing copilot. Volume: ~500k episodes/month.
Northwind's primary use case is dispatch-automation: a routing copilot with memory across drivers and shipments. Projected ingest volume is ~500k shipment-episodes per month.
Default to async Python with explicit types (AsyncStatewaveClient). No sync, no JS/TS examples.
Northwind codebase is fully async Python (FastAPI + asyncpg) and runs mypy --strict. Always show AsyncStatewaveClient with explicit type hints; never default to sync or to JS/TS examples.
Webhook URL pattern: hooks.northwind-logistics.fr/statewave/<event-type>.
Northwind webhook URLs follow the pattern hooks.northwind-logistics.fr/statewave/<event-type>. Use this pattern in any code suggestion, runbook, or example written for them.
SSO 'invalid signature' across all Northwind users → check Okta SAML cert expiry first; rotate + re-upload (~30m clear).
When Northwind reports SSO 'invalid signature' across all users, first check the SAML signing-cert expiry on their Okta IdP — the 2026-04-11 incident (#4821) was caused by an expired cert that auto-rotation didn't refresh. Resolution path: rotate cert in Okta, upload new public cert in Account → SSO. New sessions clear ~30m later.
#4821 (2026-04-11) — SSO outage, expired Okta SAML cert. Resolved in 45m, no SLA breach.
Ticket #4821 (2026-04-11): SSO outage at Northwind, all users 'invalid signature'. Root cause: expired SAML signing cert in Okta, auto-rotation didn't fire. Resolution: customer rotated cert, uploaded new public cert. Time to resolution: 45m. SLA: not breached. No code change required.
#4937 (2026-04-22) — /v1/context 30s timeouts on a 60k-memory subject. Fixed by per-region split. First-response SLA breached, service credit issued.
Ticket #4937 (2026-04-22): /v1/context timing out at 30s for Northwind. Root cause: a 60k-memory fleet subject was beyond the pgvector index sweet spot. Resolution: customer split the fleet subject into per-region subjects, latency returned to baseline. SLA breached on first response (276 minutes vs 60-minute target) — service credit issued (one prorated month). Internal action: route Northwind high-severity tickets directly to EU on-call.
High-sev Northwind tickets → page EU on-call directly (post-#4937).
Northwind high-severity tickets must page the EU on-call rotation directly. Established after #4937, where US-hours triage missed the ticket and produced a 4h+ first-response SLA breach. Do not let high-sev Northwind tickets fall through the default global queue.
Login complaints: everyone affected = SSO/Okta cert; only one group = session-cache, not SSO.
When a Northwind login complaint arrives, distinguish between SSO-wide failure (everyone affected → check Okta SAML cert per #4821) and per-group slowness (only one IdP group affected → session-cache pressure, distinct symptom). Don't conflate them; the 2026-04-18 'drivers' group slowness was unrelated to #4821.
Open feature request: per-event-type webhook filters (roadmap, no commitment).
Northwind has filed a feature request for per-event-type webhook filtering (logged 2026-04-20). Currently they receive every webhook event and filter downstream. Status: on roadmap, no committed date. If procurement makes it a blocker, escalate to product.
Backfill: 200 episodes/call, parallelized 4-wide, set last_compiled_at. ~6h for 3M events. Async Python script sent 2026-04-25.
Northwind backfill recipe: POST /v1/episodes/batch with 200 episodes per call, parallelized 4-wide. Set last_compiled_at on each batch so the compiler doesn't re-process old data. ~6 hours for their full 3M-event backfill. The runnable async Python script (mypy --strict clean) was sent 2026-04-25 and uses the hooks.northwind-logistics.fr/<event-type> webhook for progress.
Compiler: llm (switched from heuristic on 2026-04-28; recompiled existing subjects).
Northwind switched STATEWAVE_COMPILER_TYPE from heuristic to llm on 2026-04-28 because the heuristic compiler was extracting only ~5 memories per 100 shipment-event episodes. After the switch, a one-time idempotent recompile was run on existing subjects.
Renewal 2026-09-15, expanding to dock-ops team. SLA summary + quote due 2026-07-15.
Renewal scheduled for 2026-09-15 with expansion intent — Northwind plans to add their dock-ops team. Account-management to deliver an expansion quote and a quarterly SLA-performance summary (with #4937 + service credit explicitly shown) by 2026-07-15.

coding-assistant:
Stratus stack: React/TS-strict/Tailwind/react-query frontend; FastAPI/SQLModel/Pydantic v2/asyncpg backend.
Priya works on Stratus. Frontend: React + TypeScript strict mode, Tailwind utilities, react-query for client state, fetch + a thin wrapper (no axios). Backend: FastAPI + SQLModel + Pydantic v2 + asyncpg.
TS strict, no `any`, explicit types. Composition > inheritance: hooks > HOCs, protocols > ABCs.
TypeScript strict mode is enforced; never suggest `any`. Prefer explicit types and Pydantic models for backend examples. Style preference: composition over inheritance — hooks over HOCs in React, protocols + injection over ABCs in Python.
Review checklist: tests pass / no console.log / no commented-out code. Lead with these three before style.
Code-review checklist for Priya: (1) tests pass, (2) no console.log, (3) no commented-out code. Lead any review with these three checks before commenting on style. PR #458 is the canonical example — all three items flagged plus a missing test.
Integration tests > mocks. Real Postgres in CI. (Incident 2026-03-18: mocks hid migration break.)
Test policy: never mock the database. DB-touching tests run against a real Postgres in CI (pytest fixtures or pytest-postgresql). Established after a 2026-03-18 incident where mocked-DB tests passed but a `customer_id → account_id` migration shipped broken.
Top-level async + pytest-asyncio. No .then(), no asyncio.run() in library code.
Async style at Stratus: top-level async functions, await chains, no .then() chains, no asyncio.run() inside library code. Tests use pytest-asyncio with async test functions, not unittest-style + asyncio.run.
Tailwind only. No CSS-in-JS / styled-components / emotion (rolled back in 2025).
Styling: Tailwind utilities only. Don't suggest CSS-in-JS, styled-components, or emotion — Stratus tried CSS-in-JS in 2025 and rolled back due to bundle size.
ADR-007: Pricing service on Postgres + SQLModel (Mongo rejected).
Architecture decision (ADR-007, 2026-03-13): the new pricing service uses Postgres + SQLModel. Reasons: shared transactions with invoicing, existing SRE familiarity, no document-shaped writes. MongoDB was explicitly rejected.
Intermittent 500s under load → session-pool race. Fix: lock + pool_pre_ping=True + 100-concurrent invariant test (PR #389).
Fix shape from PR #389 (session-pool race): if you see intermittent 500s under concurrent load, look at session-pool acquisition first. Fix is a lock around acquire(), pool_pre_ping=True, and a 100-concurrent invariant test. The same shape may recur in other services.
Dashboards V2 work gates behind FLAG_DASHBOARD_V2 (LaunchDarkly React SDK).
Feature-flag any Dashboards V2 work behind FLAG_DASHBOARD_V2 — both client (LaunchDarkly React SDK) and server. New routes, components, and endpoints all gated.
PRs ≤150 lines; propose a stack split before exceeding that.
Diff-size convention: keep PRs concise. If a refactor exceeds ~150 lines, propose splitting into a stack of smaller PRs first. Priya prefers reviewing three small PRs over one large one.
Conventional-commits prefixes only (feat/fix/chore/refactor/test/docs).
Commit messages use conventional-commits prefixes only: feat:, fix:, chore:, refactor:, test:, docs:.
In-flight: auth-middleware → single FastAPI Depends. Blocker: migrations on import; lift to lifespan first.
Auth-middleware refactor in flight as of late April. Goal: collapse JWT verification + session-pool acquisition (currently across 4 files) into one FastAPI dependency. Blocker: one of the current files runs alembic migrations on module import (circular dep). The plan is to lift migrations into the FastAPI lifespan handler first, then extract the dependency.
Shared types in `@stratus/types` (consumed by FE + TS SDK).
Shared TS types live in the `@stratus/types` package (monorepo). Frontend and the TypeScript SDK both consume it; new domain types go there, not in app packages.
Build: pnpm + turbo. Deploy: Vercel FE, Fly.io iad+lhr BE. CI: GH Actions. Rate-limit: Redis sliding-window per API key (closed).
Build / deploy: pnpm + turbo monorepo. Frontend deploys to Vercel; backend to Fly.io in regions iad + lhr. CI is GitHub Actions. Rate-limit middleware decision: sliding-window in Redis, per-API-key (closed decision; do not re-litigate).

devops-agent:
Rollback: `fly deploy --image-label <previous>`, verify /healthz + Datadog p95 <250ms. Canary kill is faster (30s vs 6m).
Roll back the nimbus-api deploy with `fly deploy --image-label <previous>` from the nimbus repo. Verify with /healthz; check Datadog for p95 latency returning to <250ms before declaring green and unbothering the original deployer. With the canary framework (launched 2026-04-25), prefer killing the canary machine — 30s vs 6m for a full redeploy.
Regions: iad (primary) + lhr (failover). 3:1 ratio. New regions need SRE-lead approval.
nimbus-api runs in Fly regions iad (primary) and lhr (secondary failover). Adding new regions requires SRE-lead sign-off — there's a non-trivial Postgres replica cost that isn't budgeted. Scale-up keeps iad:lhr at a 3:1 ratio.
On-call: US primary / EU secondary, Mon 09:00 UTC handoff. Escalation: PagerDuty → #ops-fire → CTO at 30m P0.
On-call rotation: primary US (PT, weekdays), secondary EU (CET, weekdays + weekend coverage). Rotation handoff every Monday 09:00 UTC. Escalation: PagerDuty → Slack #ops-fire → page CTO if P0 persists past 30 minutes.
March outage (INC-2026-03-09): pool exhaustion under 5x spike. Fixed with PgBouncer + pool=50 + statement_timeout=30s. Baseline: p95 180ms, p99 450ms, 1200 RPS.
March 2026 outage post-mortem: connection-pool exhaustion under a 5x traffic spike at 14:00 UTC, ~22 minutes of timeouts. Mitigation landed: PgBouncer (transaction pooling) in front of Postgres, app-side pool raised to 50, statement_timeout=30s. Tracked as INC-2026-03-09. Post-fix capacity baseline: p95 180ms, p99 450ms, sustained 1200 RPS. Re-tune if RPS sustains >1500 for >30 minutes.
DB failover: lag-check → fly pg failover → update PGHOST → deploy → /v1/healthz?writes=1. ~6m drilled.
Postgres replica promotion runbook (last drilled 2026-03-10, 6 minutes total): (1) confirm replica lag <30s in Datadog; (2) `fly pg failover --app nimbus-pg --region lhr`; (3) update PGHOST in nimbus-api secrets to lhr-primary; (4) `fly deploy` nimbus-api to pick up the new secret; (5) verify writes via /v1/healthz?writes=1.
Secrets: 90-day rotation. Stage → /readyz → deploy. Next: 2026-06-12.
Secrets rotation cadence: every 90 days. Process: generate → `fly secrets set --stage` → verify staging /readyz → deploy. Tracked secrets: STATEWAVE_API_KEY, DATABASE_URL, DATADOG_API_KEY. Next rotation due 2026-06-12.
Backups: daily snap (14d) + WAL→S3 (30d). Monthly drills. Last RTO: 18m / 90GB.
Backup cadence: daily Postgres snapshots (14-day retention) + WAL archive shipped continuously to S3 (30-day retention). Restore drilled monthly — last successful drill 2026-03-10 with RTO 18 minutes for a 90GB instance.
Observability: Datadog (chosen over Grafana Cloud). Re-eval at 2027 bill review.
Observability stack: Datadog (logs + APM + dashboards). Chosen over Grafana Cloud because Datadog's anomaly detection caught the March outage 90 seconds before the static threshold would have. Re-evaluation gated on the 2027 bill review.
Alert thresholds: err >2%/5m, p95 >300ms/5m, p99 >800ms/5m, disk >85%/10m, lag >30s/2m. Fleet-wide, not per-host.
Standard alert thresholds (post-2026-04-08 noisy-alerts post-mortem): error rate >2% for 5m, p95 latency >300ms for 5m, p99 >800ms for 5m, disk >85% for 10m, replica lag >30s for 2m. Anything sub-5m is a graph, not a page. Alerts use fleet-wide percentiles, not per-host (single cold machines were tripping the old per-host setup).
No Friday-PM deploys. No deploys 02:00-04:00 UTC Wed. Emergency = dual sign-off.
Deploy-window policy: no Friday afternoon deploys (after 12:00 UTC). No deploys during weekly maintenance window 02:00-04:00 UTC Wednesdays. Emergency security patches exempt — but require dual sign-off in #ops-fire.
TLS: separate prod/staging Let's Encrypt issuers. Manual fallback: `fly certs add` + check. 36h auto-rotation buffer.
TLS rotation: prod and staging use separate Let's Encrypt issuer accounts (post 2026-04-15 incident where a staging-deploy storm rate-limited prod). Manual fallback if auto-rotation fails: `fly certs add <domain> --app nimbus-api`, wait for verification, then `fly certs check`. Auto-rotation triggers 36h before cert expiry, so a single failure is recoverable.
Phased migrations: additive → backfill → destructive (3 separate deploys). Combining = outage.
Migration policy: zero-downtime via alembic phased migrations — additive deploy → backfill job → destructive deploy in three separate steps. Never combine steps 1 and 3 in a single deploy. Q4 2025 incident burned us with 8 minutes of write outage from a combined migration.
SLA: 99.95% monthly. Current: 99.99%. Canary auto-rollback at >0.5% err = 30s rollback.
SLA target: 99.95% monthly uptime for nimbus-api (excludes scheduled maintenance windows). Current month tracking 99.99% — one 4-minute incident 2026-04-15 (TLS rotation, no customer impact). Canary deploy framework (launched 2026-04-25) reduced last-mile rollback from 6 minutes to 30 seconds via auto-rollback on >0.5% error rate.
Volume-reattach restart → 30s graceful shutdown drain. Without it: ~40 lost requests.
When fly machines auto-restart on volume reattach (e.g. OS upgrade), give the nimbus-api graceful-shutdown handler up to 30s to drain in-flight requests — confirmed 2026-04-19 incident where a routine iad-3 OS upgrade lost ~40 in-flight requests without it. Fly support confirmed this is expected behavior on volume reattach.

research-assistant:
APA 7th, DOI required, arXiv abs (not PDF), no blog posts in main lit review.
Citation style: APA 7th edition. Always include the DOI when available; for preprints, link the arXiv abs URL (not the PDF). Don't cite blog posts in the formal lit review — they go in a separate appendix file.
Research area: agent memory. Reading list: RAG, mech interp, agent memory. Scope: post-2022 LLM-era.
Arushi's active research area: agent memory architectures. Reading list this quarter: retrieval-augmented generation, mechanistic interpretability, agent memory architectures. Lit-review scope is post-2022 LLM-era only — older work goes in the historical-context appendix.
Hypothesis: structured + deterministic memory > naïve RAG. Empirically: 0.81 vs 0.34 vs 0.62 (April 28 experiment).
Working hypothesis: structured, typed memory with deterministic ranking outperforms naïve RAG on multi-session evaluation tasks. Key claims: (a) provenance enables eval reproducibility, (b) recency × kind-priority handles temporal validity better than cosine similarity. Empirically supported by the multi-session-recall experiment (2026-04-28): Statewave 0.81, naïve RAG 0.34, history-stuffing 0.62.
Co-author: Dr. Mei Wu (UCB, PT). Async review only. (MIT collab declined Nov 2025.)
Sole co-author for the active NeurIPS submission: Dr. Mei Wu (UC Berkeley, PT timezone). Sole co-author after the MIT collaborator declined in November 2025. Mei prefers async review — comments on shared drafts, no live calls unless flagged urgent.
Cited: Park 2024 (Generative Agents, retrieval-time grading), Chen 2025 (memory taxonomy survey).
Key papers cited in the lit review so far: Park et al. 2024 'Generative Agents' (DOI 10.1145/3586183.3606763) — memory-stream scoring, retrieval-time LLM grading vs Statewave's compile-time grading. Chen 2025 'Survey of Memory Architectures for Autonomous Agents' (arXiv:2501.04822) — episodic/semantic/procedural/working taxonomy, used to frame section 2.
Draft: NeurIPS 2026. Done: §1, §2, §4. §3 = hard cutoffs (Mei agreed 2026-04-15). Sigmoid decay in subsection.
Active draft: 'Scoring Memories with Provenance: A Determinism-First Approach to Agent Memory' (NeurIPS 2026 target). Sections done: 1 (intro), 2 (related work), 4 (architecture). Section 3 (formalisation) settled on hard temporal-validity cutoffs for cleaner determinism proofs; sigmoid decay relegated to a 'Practical extensions' subsection. Tools: Zotero + BibTeX, Obsidian, Markdown → LaTeX via pandoc. NOT Mendeley/Notion.

Explicit imports, no save-time auto-format. Format only at submission.

compiler-density (Feb 25): LLM 2× density, 1.7× recall vs heuristic on operational data.
Experiment 'compiler-density' (2026-02-25): heuristic vs LLM compilation on a 200-episode synthetic operational dataset. Heuristic ~5 memories per 100 episodes, recall@5 0.42. LLM ~10 memories per 100 episodes, recall@5 0.71. LLM is ~2× density and ~1.7× recall on operational data — justifies the cost.

Section 1 wedge: deterministic ranking → reproducible eval; vector-only ≠ deterministic.
Core argument for NeurIPS paper section 1: ranking determinism implies eval reproducibility. If (subject, task, budget) always returns the same context bundle, retrieval regression tests are well-defined. Vector-only systems can't make that claim because index state varies. This is the wedge against naïve RAG.

arushi
Experiment 'multi-session-recall': 50-session benchmark built per the January hypothesis. Statewave: 0.81 recall@5 on prior-state retrieval; naïve RAG (cosine top-5 over message chunks): 0.34; full history stuffing (no retrieval, just concat): 0.62 but exhausts 8k token budget. Statewave wins on quality + budget; naïve RAG fails on temporal recall.
arushi

arushi
Section 3 progress: settled on hard cutoff for the formalisation (cleaner proofs of determinism), sigmoid decay relegated to a subsection 'Practical extensions.' Mei reviewed and agrees — async comment on the shared draft.
arushi
Open question, still unresolved: should temporal validity be expressed as a hard cutoff (`valid_from`/`valid_to` boundaries flip a memory in/out of the bundle) or a sigmoid decay (memory weight smoothly degrades past `valid_to`)? Hard cutoff is more deterministic; sigmoid decay handles 'mostly stale but still informative' better. Probably need both — model dependent.
arushi
Reading queue (in priority order): (1) Liu et al. 2025 on subject-design patterns for multi-tenant agent memory, (2) Voigt 2026 preprint on temporal validity in semantic memory, (3) Tanaka 2025 on idempotent compilation. Plan to finish queue by 2026-04-25 and incorporate citations into section 2.
arushi
Insight: ranking determinism implies eval reproducibility — if same subject + same task + same budget always returns the same context bundle, then a regression test on retrieval is well-defined. Vector-only systems can't make that claim because index state varies. This is the core argument for the NeurIPS paper's section 1.

Result: heuristic extracts ~5 memories per 100 episodes; LLM extracts ~10. Recall@5 on prior-state retrieval: heuristic 0.42, LLM 0.71. LLM ~2× density and ~1.7× recall on operational data.

In progress: section 3 (formalisation of the ranking function). Section 3 blocked on whether to express temporal validity as a hard cutoff or a sigmoid decay — undecided.

Read: Chen 2025, 'A Survey of Memory Architectures for Autonomous Agents.' arXiv:2501.04822. Takeaway: surveys 23 systems, taxonomy of episodic/semantic/procedural/working memory. Our framing aligns with their typology. Cite in section 2.

Read: Park et al. 2024, 'Generative Agents: Interactive Simulacra of Human Behavior.' DOI 10.1145/3586183.3606763. Takeaway: their 'memory stream' uses recency × importance × relevance scoring — same shape as Statewave's ranking but they use LLM scoring at retrieval time (we score at compile time). Useful precedent for the determinism argument.

arushi
Working hypothesis: structured, typed memory with deterministic ranking outperforms naïve RAG on multi-session evaluation tasks because (a) provenance enables eval reproducibility and (b) recency × kind-priority weighting handles temporal validity better than pure cosine similarity. To test: build a 50-session benchmark.

Active reading list this quarter: retrieval-augmented generation, mechanistic interpretability, and agent memory architectures. Lit-review scope is post-2022 LLM-era only — anything earlier goes in the historical-context appendix, not the main review.

Steps to merge too-fine subjects in Statewave.
To merge too-fine subjects in Statewave, pick a canonical destination, use /admin/memory/clone to copy memories, recompile, and delete the source subjects.
Steps to split too-coarse subjects in Statewave.
To split too-coarse subjects in Statewave, use /admin/memory/clone with a filter to copy memories, re-run compile, and delete the source rows afterward.
Statewave subjects should be persistent entities, not sessions.
Statewave requires a persistent entity (user/account/project) as the subject, with session metadata for episodes.
Tenant_id should be used for better isolation instead of tenant-as-prefix.
Using tenant_id is preferred over tenant-as-prefix to maintain row-level isolation.
Emails should not be used as subject IDs due to migration issues.
Stable surrogate IDs (like DB primary keys, UUIDs, or slugs) should be used instead of email as subject IDs.
Memory should not be split by topic; one subject per user is best.
One subject per user is recommended to avoid splitting memory across topics.
Subjects should not be created per LLM call; they should represent entities.
Each call should not create a new subject; the subject should be the entity, not the call.
Agent personality should not split memory; it belongs in the prompt template.
Personality should be included in the prompt template, not as a separate subject.
Avoid mega-subjects to maintain effective memory retrieval.
Memory retrieval can become diluted with a mega-subject; subjects should be split by entity.
Statewave is intended for persistent memory across sessions.
Statewave is designed for memory across sessions, not for stateless interactions.
Using a single subject per user is generally the best practice.
Single subject per user is almost always the right approach for memory management.
Service incidents belong to the service subject; policies to the conventions subject.
Service incidents and team-wide policies should be documented under their respective subjects.
Accounts should have individual subjects for effective tracking.
Each account in the pipeline should have its own subject to track decision-makers and preferences.
Agent memory primarily revolves around the codebase and its conventions.
Most agent memory is focused on the codebase, including stack choices and conventions.
Support interactions are linked to specific customer subjects.
Customer support interactions should be recorded under the respective customer subject.
Appending to subjects is preferred unless retrieval becomes confusing.
When in doubt, append to the existing subject rather than creating a new one.
Sessions belong in episode metadata, not as separate subjects.
Sessions should not be treated as subjects; they should be part of the episode metadata.
Statewave subjects lack hierarchical structure.
Statewave subjects are flat and do not have a built-in parent_subject_id.
Hierarchy is represented through subject id conventions and separate writes.
Hierarchy in Statewave is modeled by convention in the subject id and separate writes to relevant levels.
Stable ids like tenant-catalogue are used for tenant-wide knowledge.
For tenant-wide knowledge, use a stable id like tenant-catalogue and write under the tenant's tenant_id.
Tenant_id is used for enforcing isolation in multi-tenant applications.
In a multi-tenant application, use tenant_id to enforce data isolation.
Tenancy should not be encoded in subject ids; use tenant_id instead.
Do not encode tenancy into the subject id; use tenant_id for tenancy and subject_id for the memory's subject.
Delete by subject serves as the boundary for GDPR-style erasure.
Statewave's 'delete by subject' is the GDPR-style erasure boundary.
Evaluate subject scheme by asking five context-related questions.
Before committing to a subject scheme, write down five questions your agent should handle and determine if the same entity is needed for context.

Subjects are automatically created when the first episode is written.
Subjects are created implicitly on first episode write.
Each episode and memory is associated with a specific subject id.
Every episode and memory carries the subject id.
All data associated with a subject is removed when the subject is deleted.
Deleting a subject deletes all its data.
Tenancy in Statewave is managed through tenant_id rather than subject id.
Tenancy is enforced by tenant_id on episodes/memories, not by the subject id.
Users can define the format of their subject ids in Statewave.

The procedure outlines how Statewave ingests events, compiles memories, retrieves context, and manages data.
1. Ingest raw events (episodes) — conversations, tool calls, decisions, observations. 2. Compile durable memories — typed, summarised, confidence-scored, with provenance back to source episodes. 3. Retrieve relevant context — filtered, scored, and token-budgeted for your prompt. 4. Inspect and delete by subject — full timeline visibility and GDPR-style erasure.
Statewave serves as a memory runtime designed for AI agents and applications.
Statewave is a memory runtime for AI agents and AI-powered applications.
Docker is a necessary component for running Statewave.
Statewave requires Docker for its operation.

Node ≥18 is required to run the operator console and marketing/demo website locally.

To remove all data for a subject, use the delete_subject method with the subject ID.
The procedure explains how to search for memories based on kind or text.
To find specific memories by kind or text, use the search_memories method with the user ID and desired kind.
Retrieving user timeline and memory count.
This episode discusses how to retrieve the timeline of episodes and memories for a specific user.
Context endpoint assembles ranked bundles for AI tasks.
The context endpoint assembles a ranked, token-bounded bundle for AI tasks, prioritizing facts, procedures, summaries, and raw episodes.
Compilation extracts structured memories with provenance.
Compilation extracts structured memories from raw episodes, including facts, summaries, and procedures, with provenance back to the source.
Episodes are the raw interaction records in Statewave.
Episodes are raw interaction records that Statewave remembers.

Exceeding the rate limit returns 429 with a Retry-After header.
Health endpoints are not subject to rate limiting.
Health endpoints (/healthz, /readyz) are exempt from rate limiting.

NULL-tenant rows are excluded from tenant_id filtered queries.
Queries with a tenant_id filter will NOT return NULL-tenant rows.
Single-tenant mode returns all rows without filtering by tenant_id.
Queries without a tenant_id will return all rows regardless of tenant_id value.

Queries are tenant-scoped when tenant requirement is enabled.

Data isolation prevents tenants from accessing each other's data.
Tenant A cannot read, search, or delete tenant B's data.
Admin endpoints allow optional tenant filtering but are accessible to operators.
Admin endpoints can optionally filter by tenant but are not restricted.
Requests require a valid API key when STATEWAVE_API_KEY is set, with some exceptions.
When STATEWAVE_API_KEY is configured, all requests must include a valid API key except for certain endpoints.
Requests without authentication get a 401 error, while invalid keys get a 403 error.

Statewave requires PostgreSQL as its database backend.
PostgreSQL is required as the backend for Statewave.
Statewave is designed to run in a single-node configuration without clustering.
Statewave operates in single-node mode with no clustering.
There is no built-in authentication provider in Statewave.
Statewave does not have a built-in auth provider.
Rate limiting applies to individual IPs rather than tenants or API keys.
Rate limiting is per-IP and not per-tenant or per-API-key.
Statewave's responsible disclosure policy outlines their approach to security research.
Statewave believes in responsible disclosure and will work with you to understand and validate the issue, keep you informed of progress, credit researchers who report valid issues, and not take legal action against good-faith security research.
Statewave employs multiple security measures to maintain system integrity.
Statewave maintains security through dependency scanning, code scanning, CI/CD security, secret management, access control, and audit logging.
Subject size limits in Statewave affect performance when exceeding certain thresholds.
Statewave does not have a hard cap on subject size, but latency starts climbing past a few tens of thousands of memories per subject.
Subject deletion in Statewave is permanent and requires caution.
Deleting a subject in Statewave is irreversible and removes all episodes, memories, and embeddings for that subject.
Statewave subjects are not enumerated and must be managed by the application.
Statewave does not provide a listing endpoint for subjects; subjects are identified by the application.
A structured approach for customer onboarding involves specific recall questions and subject management.
For customer onboarding, start with a recall test that includes questions about the customer and known onboarding blockers, using per-customer and cross-cutting subjects.
AI support agents often lack memory, leading to degraded context and repeated information requests.

AI agents struggle with memory, causing issues in recognizing returning customers and recalling past interactions.

Concatenating conversation history into prompts leads to inefficiencies and limitations in AI support agents.

Statewave's context assembly respects a configurable token budget.

Compilation in Statewave is idempotent, producing no duplicate memories.

Statewave organizes everything around subjects, covering the full lifecycle from ingest to delete.

https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/deployment/migrations.md#stuck-compilation-jobs-after-upgrade
Some in-flight jobs may be orphaned after an upgrade. The `/readyz` endpoint will report them as "degraded". They will self-recover after 30 minutes, or you can manually reset them:

```sql
UPDATE compilation_jobs SET status = 'pending', claimed_at = NULL
WHERE status = 'running' AND claimed_at < NOW() - INTERVAL '30 minutes';
```

https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/dev/backup-restore.md#full-database-backup-postgres-native
For complete disaster recovery, use Postgres tools:

```bash
# Backup
pg_dump -h localhost -U statewave statewave > statewave-full-backup.sql

# Restore
psql -h localhost -U statewave statewave < statewave-full-backup.sql
```

Use full DB backups for:
- Disaster recovery
- Before schema migrations
- Environment cloning
- Point-in-time recovery (with WAL archiving)

Statewave's subject export/import does NOT replace pg_dump. It complements it for subject-level operations.

```bash
curl -X POST http://localhost:8100/admin/import \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "document": '"$(cat user-123-backup.json)"',
    "target_subject_id": "user-123-restored",
    "preserve_ids": true
  }'
```

Options:

| Option | Default | Description |
|--------|---------|-------------|
| `target_subject_id` | original | Override the subject_id on import |
| `target_tenant_id` | original | Override the tenant_id on import |
| `preserve_ids` | `true` | Keep original UUIDs (false = generate new) |

https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/openai-assistants.md#migrating-from-the-openai-assistants-api
> **TL;DR** — The Assistants API was deprecated on **August 26, 2025** and is **scheduled for sunset on August 26, 2026** ([OpenAI deprecations page](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/deprecations)). OpenAI's recommended migration path is the **Responses API** + the **Conversations API**. That migration is itself a good moment to reconsider whether your "memory layer" should be tied to a single LLM provider at all. This page lays out both paths — Responses API (stay with OpenAI's stack) and Statewave (vendor-neutral memory layer) — and when each makes sense.

References
comparisons/openai-assistants.md
Migrating from the OpenAI Assistants API › References
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/openai-assistants.md#side-by-side
| | **Responses + Conversations API** | **Statewave** |
|---|---|---|
| **What it is** | OpenAI's successor to the Assistants API | Self-hosted vendor-neutral memory runtime |
| **Vendor lock-in** | OpenAI-only. Your conversation items live in OpenAI's infrastructure. | None — call any LLM (Anthropic, Mistral, Bedrock, Ollama, Google, OpenAI) for completions; memory stays in your Postgres. |
| **Memory model** | Conversation items (messages, tool calls, tool outputs); state across calls via conversation chaining | Episodes (immutable raw events) → typed Memories (compiled, scored, with provenance) → ranked, token-bounded Context bundles |
| **Cross-session continuity** | A conversation persists; long-term identity facts about a user across many conversations are not first-class | First-class. Subjects (per user / account / workspace) accumulate facts and procedures across every session |
| **Ranked retrieval** | Implicit in the conversation history's recency; no exposed ranking signals | Explicit: kind priority × recency × task relevance × temporal validity × semantic similarity, with `source_episode_ids` returned per fact |
| **Token-bounded context** | The completion API handles context windowing; managed by OpenAI | First-class: `max_tokens` parameter, `token_estimate` returned with every bundle |
| **Provenance** | Conversation items carry their own metadata; OpenAI doesn't expose a fact-to-source citation chain | Every memory traces to its source episodes by ID |
| **Self-hosted option** | No (OpenAI-managed) | Yes (only) |
| **Dependency on a model provider** | Hard — OpenAI is the platform | None — Statewave's heuristic compiler is fully local; LLM compilation routes through LiteLLM (100+ providers) |

---
Side-by-side
comparisons/openai-assistants.md
Migrating from the OpenAI Assistants API › Side-by-side
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/openai-assistants.md#when-responses-conversations-api-alone-is-the-right-call
- Your application is and will remain **OpenAI-first**. You've factored OpenAI as a strategic dependency and a single migration is acceptable.
- Your memory needs are **session-shaped**, not subject-shaped. Conversations naturally end; you don't have a *"what did Alice tell me six months ago"* requirement.
- You don't need **fact-level provenance** for compliance or product reasons.
- Your team values **a single hosted stack** and the Responses + Conversations APIs cover that.
- Your traffic and budget make the OpenAI Conversations storage costs reasonable for your retention requirements.
When Responses + Conversations API alone is the right call
comparisons/openai-assistants.md
Migrating from the OpenAI Assistants API › When Responses + Conversations API alone is the right call
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/openai-assistants.md#when-moving-memory-to-statewave-or-any-vendor-neutral-layer-is-the-right-call
- **Multi-provider strategy.** You want to call Claude for some tasks, Gemini for others, a local Llama for cheap ones. Memory must follow the user, not the provider. Statewave is provider-agnostic at the memory layer; the model swap is a one-line change in your prompt code.
- **Self-hosted is required.** Data residency, compliance, or a vendor-restriction policy precludes OpenAI hosting your memory.
- **Long-term identity matters.** Customers return weeks or months later. The conversation thread is the *wrong* unit — you need *subject* memory that persists across many threads. Statewave is built around subjects; the Responses API is built around conversations.
- **Eval-driven memory matters.** When the agent's reply changes between runs, you need to know whether your retrieval changed or whether the model changed. Statewave's deterministic retrieval gives you a regression test surface; OpenAI's hosted memory doesn't expose one.
- **Provenance for support and trust.** *"Show me the conversations that produced this fact about the customer"* is a load-bearing query in support workflows. Statewave's `source_episode_ids` makes that two clicks; OpenAI's APIs don't surface it as a first-class chain.

When moving memory to Statewave (or any vendor-neutral layer) is the right call
comparisons/openai-assistants.md
Migrating from the OpenAI Assistants API › When moving memory to Statewave (or any vendor-neutral layer) is the right call
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/openai-assistants.md#migration-recipe-assistants-api-statewave
This is *one* path. The other path (Assistants → Responses + Conversations) is documented in [OpenAI's migration guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/migrate-to-responses); the two aren't exclusive — you'll move to the Responses API for completions either way.
Migration recipe — Assistants API → Statewave
comparisons/openai-assistants.md
Migrating from the OpenAI Assistants API › Migration recipe — Assistants API → Statewave
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/openai-assistants.md#choosing-the-subject
The hard step is identifying the *subject*. Don't make threads your subjects — that loses the cross-session value you're migrating for. Pick the persistent entity (user, account, workspace, agent role) whose history you'd want recalled together. See [subject-design.md](../subject-design.md) for the full pattern guide.
Choosing the subject
comparisons/openai-assistants.md
Migrating from the OpenAI Assistants API › Migration recipe — Assistants API → Statewave › Choosing the subject
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/openai-assistants.md#migration-steps
1. **Stand up Statewave.** Single-Postgres + a container; ~10 minutes via [getting-started.md](../getting-started.md).
2. **For each Assistants thread you want to preserve**, identify its subject (typically the authenticated user — `subject_id = f"user-{user_id}"`).
3. **Bulk-export thread messages** via the Assistants API while it's still alive.
4. **Replay each message as a Statewave episode** under the right subject:

   ```python
   sw.add_episode(
       subject_id=f"user-{user_id}",
       source="chat",
       type="conversation",
       payload={"messages": [{"role": msg.role, "content": msg.content[0].text.value}]},
       metadata={
           "session_id": thread.id,           # preserve thread identity for traceability
           "migrated_from": "openai-assistants",
           "original_message_id": msg.id,
       },
       created_at=msg.created_at,             # preserve original timestamp
   )
   ```

5. **Run `/v1/memories/compile` per migrated subject** — Statewave extracts profile facts, summaries, and procedures from the imported episodes.
6. **Cut over your application's prompt-assembly path** to call Statewave for context instead of relying on OpenAI's thread state:

   ```python
   bundle = sw.get_context(subject_id=f"user-{user_id}", task=user_question, max_tokens=600)
   prompt = bundle.assembled_context + "\n\nUser: " + user_question
   reply = openai.responses.create(model="gpt-4o-mini", input=prompt)
   ```

7. **Verify on representative queries** before turning off Assistants API access. Spot-check that retrieved facts match what the old agent recalled.

What you start paying for
comparisons/openai-assistants.md
Migrating from the OpenAI Assistants API › Migration recipe — Assistants API → Statewave › What you start paying for
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/openai-assistants.md#a-pragmatic-middle-path
You don't have to migrate to Statewave to escape the Assistants API sunset. The realistic deadline-driven path:

1. **Migrate completions** from Assistants API to Responses API (per OpenAI's migration guide). This unblocks the August 2026 sunset.
2. **Separately, on your own timeline**, decide whether to keep memory in OpenAI (Conversations API) or move it to a vendor-neutral layer (Statewave or otherwise). This decision is independent of the deprecation deadline — Conversations API works fine.

Bundling both decisions creates risk of slipping the sunset deadline. Decoupling them lets you ship the urgent thing first and re-evaluate the architectural thing under less pressure.

---
A pragmatic middle path
comparisons/openai-assistants.md
Migrating from the OpenAI Assistants API › A pragmatic middle path
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/openai-assistants.md#honest-gaps
- **Statewave is not a chat platform.** It doesn't run conversations, manage tool calls, host files, or invoke models. Your application code (or an SDK like the OpenAI Agents SDK) does those things; Statewave just supplies context.
- **You still need a completion API.** Migrating away from the Assistants API doesn't mean leaving OpenAI. Most Statewave users keep using OpenAI (or another provider) via the Responses API for completions; Statewave just means the *memory* is portable.
- **No managed plane.** OpenAI's hosted memory is operationally simple — you don't run anything. Statewave is self-hosted, period. Factor the ops cost honestly.

## 3. Single Container Deployment

Deploy Statewave as a single container pointing to an external Postgres.

```bash
docker build -t statewave .

docker run -d \
  --name statewave \
  -p 8100:8100 \
  -e STATEWAVE_DATABASE_URL=postgresql+asyncpg://user:pass@your-db-host:5432/statewave \
  -e STATEWAVE_DEBUG=false \
  -e STATEWAVE_API_KEY=your-secret-key \
  statewave
```

Run migrations against the remote database before first start:

```bash
docker run --rm \
  -e STATEWAVE_DATABASE_URL=postgresql+asyncpg://user:pass@your-db-host:5432/statewave \
  statewave \
  alembic upgrade head
```

Hardware & Scaling
deployment/hardware-and-scaling.md
Hardware & Scaling
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/deployment/hardware-and-scaling.md#gpu-requirements
**Statewave itself never requires a GPU.** The server is a stateless FastAPI process that talks to Postgres + pgvector — both CPU-only.

GPU only enters the picture if **you choose** to:

| Choice | GPU implication |
|--------|-----------------|
| Run the **heuristic compiler** (default) | None. Pure regex. CPU only. |
| Run the **LLM compiler** with a hosted provider (OpenAI, Anthropic, Bedrock, …) | None for you. The provider runs the model. |
| Run the **LLM compiler** with a self-hosted model (Ollama, vLLM, TGI, …) | A GPU is typically required wherever that model runs — but that's separate infrastructure, not Statewave. |
| Use **hosted embeddings** (OpenAI, Cohere, …) | None for you. |
| Use a **self-hosted embedding model** | GPU may improve throughput — same caveat: separate infrastructure. |

In short: the Statewave container is CPU-only and is happy on a `shared-cpu-1x` Fly machine, a small Railway instance, or a 1-CPU container on Kubernetes. GPU questions belong to the model-hosting layer you configure, if any.

---
GPU requirements
deployment/hardware-and-scaling.md
Hardware & Scaling › GPU requirements
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/deployment/hardware-and-scaling.md#what-weve-validated
- **Single-node deployments** work well for the design point: small-to-medium support / coding / sales agents with thousands of subjects.
- Compilation, retrieval, and ingest are all I/O-bound on Postgres for typical workloads.
- The LLM compiler caps in-flight requests at **4** to avoid hammering providers; this is the dominant throughput limit when LLM compilation is on.
What we've validated
deployment/hardware-and-scaling.md
Hardware & Scaling › Scaling › What we've validated
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/deployment/hardware-and-scaling.md#operator-guidance
- **Postgres is the bottleneck.** Connection pool size, vacuum cadence, and pgvector index health matter more than API CPU.
- **LLM compiler latency dominates** when enabled — episode-batch round-trips to the provider determine how fast new memories appear. Heuristic compilation is essentially free in comparison.
- **Embeddings**: if you generate embeddings synchronously on every memory write, embedding-provider latency is in your write path. Consider batching or async generation if write volume is high.
- **Rate limiting** is per-IP, sliding-window, Postgres-backed — shared correctly across multiple workers.
- **Tenant isolation** is app-layer (`tenant_id` scoping in queries). It is not row-level-security; if you have hard-isolation requirements, run separate databases per tenant.

deployment/troubleshooting.md
Deployment Troubleshooting
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/deployment/hardware-and-scaling.md#compiler-choice-and-scaling
| Compiler | Throughput characteristic | What scales it |
|----------|---------------------------|----------------|
| Heuristic | High; CPU-bound, deterministic per-episode cost | API workers + Postgres write throughput |
| LLM (hosted provider) | Bound by provider rate limits and the in-flight cap of 4 | Provider quota; tune `STATEWAVE_LLM_*` settings |
| LLM (self-hosted) | Bound by your model server's throughput | GPU/inference server capacity |

Pick the compiler that matches your scale and privacy needs first. Don't conflate "we want LLM-quality extraction" with "we need GPUs" — those are decoupled by your choice of provider.

deployment/hardware-and-scaling.md
Hardware & Scaling › Compiler choice and scaling
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/deployment/troubleshooting.md#statewave-ts-001-database-connection-timeout-rate-limiter
**Symptoms:**

- API requests hang or return 502/504
- Logs show `distributed_rate_limit_db_error` warnings
- `TimeoutError` in asyncpg connection stack traces
- `asyncio.exceptions.CancelledError` in rate limit code path

**Example log:**

```
distributed_rate_limit_db_error key=172.16.22.98 request_id=...
TimeoutError
```

**Root cause:**

The distributed rate limiter (`STATEWAVE_RATE_LIMIT_STRATEGY=distributed`) opens a database connection for every incoming request to check/increment a counter. On constrained Postgres instances (e.g., Fly.io shared Postgres with limited connection slots), this exhausts the connection pool. Once the pool is full, actual API queries (episodes, memories, search) cannot acquire connections and time out.

**Fix:**

Switch to in-memory rate limiting if running a single instance:

```bash
fly secrets set STATEWAVE_RATE_LIMIT_STRATEGY=memory
```

**When to use distributed:**

Only use `distributed` when:
- Running multiple app instances that need shared counters
- Your Postgres has sufficient connection headroom (pool_size + rate_limit traffic)
- You've configured a separate connection pool or Redis for rate limiting

**When to use memory:**

Use `memory` (recommended for single-instance deployments) when:
- Running 1 machine on Fly.io
- Using shared/limited Postgres
- Rate limit accuracy across restarts isn't critical

**Prevention:**

If you need distributed rate limiting without DB pressure, consider:
- Dedicated Redis for rate limiting (future)
- Separate connection pool with lower timeout for rate limit queries
- Increasing Postgres `max_connections`

deployment/troubleshooting.md

https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/deployment/troubleshooting.md#statewave-ts-002-missing-database-column
**Symptoms:**

- Health check shows `"status": "degraded"` for queue
- `UndefinedColumnError: column "X" does not exist`

**Root cause:**

A migration added a column that wasn't applied to the production database, or the column was added in code but the migration wasn't created yet.

**Fix:**

Run migrations:

```bash
fly ssh console -C "alembic upgrade head"
```

Or manually add the column:

```bash
fly ssh console -C "python -c \"
import asyncio, os
from sqlalchemy.ext.asyncio import create_async_engine
from sqlalchemy import text

async def main():
    e = create_async_engine(os.environ['DATABASE_URL'])
    async with e.begin() as conn:
        await conn.execute(text('ALTER TABLE <table> ADD COLUMN IF NOT EXISTS <column> <type>'))
    await e.dispose()

asyncio.run(main())
\""
```

https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/deployment/migrations.md#overview
Statewave uses [Alembic](https://alembic.sqlalchemy.org/) for database migrations. Migrations are applied via `alembic upgrade head` and are **forward-only** in normal operation.

The app includes safety features:
- **Preflight script** — verifies DB reachability and migration state before you commit
- **Startup schema guard** — warns (or blocks) if the app's expected schema doesn't match the DB
- **`/ops/migrations` endpoint** — live introspection of migration state

https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/deployment/troubleshooting.md#statewave-ts-003-cors-errors-from-frontend
**Symptoms:**

- Browser console shows `Access to fetch blocked by CORS policy`
- `access-control-allow-origin` header missing from responses
- Website or admin console can't reach the API

**Root cause:**

`STATEWAVE_CORS_ORIGINS` doesn't include the requesting domain.

**Fix:**

Update the origins list (JSON array):

```bash
fly secrets set 'STATEWAVE_CORS_ORIGINS=["https://admin.statewave.ai","https://www.statewave.ai","https://statewave.ai","http://localhost:5173"]'
```

**Verify:**

```bash
curl -I -X OPTIONS "https://statewave-api.fly.dev/healthz" \
  -H "Origin: https://admin.statewave.ai" \
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: GET"
# Should include: access-control-allow-origin: https://admin.statewave.ai
```

**Symptoms:**

- Admin dashboard shows "Failed to reach backend"
- `/api/proxy?path=/admin/dashboard` returns `{"error": "STATEWAVE_API_URL not configured"}`

**Root cause:**

Vercel environment variables (`STATEWAVE_API_URL`, `STATEWAVE_API_KEY`) are not set for the project.

**Fix:**

```bash
cd statewave-admin
vercel env add STATEWAVE_API_URL production   # https://statewave-api.fly.dev
vercel env add STATEWAVE_API_KEY production   # your API key
vercel --prod --yes                           # redeploy
```

Migration & Upgrade Runbook
deployment/migrations.md
Migration & Upgrade Runbook
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/openai-assistants.md#where-things-stand-today-may-2026
From OpenAI's announcements and migration guide:

> **"Assistants API beta deprecation — August 26, 2026 sunset."**
>
> *"When OpenAI released the Responses API in March 2025, they announced plans to bring all Assistants API features to the easier to use Responses API, with a sunset date in 2026. After achieving feature parity in the Responses API, OpenAI has deprecated the Assistants API."*

If you're running on the Assistants API today, you have a hard deadline. The two questions are *what to migrate to* and *what to take the chance to fix while you're already touching the integration*.

The Responses API + Conversations API replaces Threads + Messages with a different shape:

| Assistants API | Responses API + Conversations API |
|---|---|
| Thread (collection of messages, server-side) | Conversation (collection of items: messages, tool calls, tool outputs, other data) |
| `previous_response_id` chaining | Manage conversations as first-class objects |
| Threads-as-memory | Conversations-as-state; long-term memory not provided |

Per OpenAI's [migration guide](https://platform.openai.com/docs/guides/migrate-to-responses), the new shape *"moves to a simpler mental model where you send input items and get output items back."*
Where things stand today (May 2026)
comparisons/openai-assistants.md
Migrating from the OpenAI Assistants API › Where things stand today (May 2026)
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/openai-assistants.md#the-decision-the-migration-forces
When the deprecation fired, every Assistants-API user inherited a choice:

1. **Migrate to Responses + Conversations API.** Stay with OpenAI's stack, port your thread-based code to conversation items, ship.
2. **Move memory out of the model provider entirely.** Use the Responses API (or any other LLM API) for completions, but persist memory in a vendor-neutral layer. Statewave is one such layer; there are others.

Both are valid. The right choice depends on whether your application's persistent memory is **bound to OpenAI or not**.

The decision the migration forces
comparisons/openai-assistants.md
Migrating from the OpenAI Assistants API › The decision the migration forces
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/openai-assistants.md#conceptual-mapping
| Assistants API concept | Statewave concept |
|---|---|
| Assistant (an AI personality + tools) | Stays in your application code. Statewave doesn't model assistants — they're an orchestration concern. |
| Thread (a conversation) | Episodes on a subject, scoped by `metadata.session_id`. The subject is the user/account, *not* the thread. |
| Message in a thread | A `payload.messages` entry on an episode |
| `assistant.tools` configuration | Stays in your code (Responses API tool config, OpenAI Agents SDK tool definitions, etc.) |
| File attachments / `file_ids` | Statewave doesn't host files. Carry references in episode metadata; store the file content wherever you do today. |

### Steps

1. Connect your `statewave` repo to Railway
2. Add a **Postgres** service (Railway provisions pgvector-capable Postgres)

   - `STATEWAVE_DATABASE_URL` → Railway provides `DATABASE_URL`; convert to asyncpg format: replace `postgresql://` with `postgresql+asyncpg://`

## Admin Console (optional)

For operator visibility into your running Statewave instance, you can deploy the admin console.

See [statewave-admin](https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-admin) for setup instructions.

The admin console provides:
- System readiness and database health status
- Compile job monitoring
- Webhook delivery status
- Usage metering (episodes, memories, compiles)
- Subject health distribution

**Important:** The admin console is an internal tool. Deploy it behind an access gateway (Cloudflare Access, OAuth2 Proxy, etc.) — do not expose publicly.

Steps
deployment/guide.md
Deployment Guide › 1. Local Development (Docker Compose) › Steps
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/langchain-memory.md#honest-gaps
- **Statewave doesn't orchestrate.** If you don't want a graph framework, fine — but you'll need *some* loop. LangGraph is one good choice; the OpenAI Agents SDK and Vercel AI SDK are others. Statewave doesn't replace any of them.
- **No graph state persistence.** Within-thread state is LangGraph's job; Statewave doesn't try to do it. Don't write graph state into Statewave episodes.
- **More moving parts.** A LangGraph + Statewave deployment has two services to operate (LangGraph's chosen checkpoint store + Statewave's Postgres). For simple cases the operational overhead isn't justified.
- **Different mental models.** LangGraph thinks in *graphs* and *threads*; Statewave thinks in *subjects* and *episodes*. Teams need to be comfortable holding both.

---
Honest gaps
comparisons/langchain-memory.md
Statewave vs LangChain / LangGraph Memory › Honest gaps
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/langchain-memory.md#when-statewave-alongside-langgraph-is-the-right-call
- **Your agent runs across many sessions** and the value is in long-term memory: identity facts, preferences, prior issues, learned procedures. Building ranking + budgeting + provenance over `Store` is real work — Statewave ships it.
- **You want one memory layer across multiple agent stacks.** A LangGraph support agent + an OpenAI-Agents-SDK sales copilot + a custom Express coding assistant should share one memory surface. HTTP-API memory works everywhere; LangGraph `Store` only works inside LangGraph.
- **Eval-driven memory matters.** Statewave's deterministic retrieval is regression-testable. LangGraph's `store.search()` plus your scoring code is testable too — but you write all that yourself.
- **Provenance is a compliance or product requirement.** When a customer asks "why did the agent say this about my account?" you need a finite, queryable chain. Statewave's `source_episode_ids → episodes` is that chain by default.
- **Memory operations should outlive the agent framework.** If you migrate from LangGraph to a different orchestrator next year, your subject memory survives — it lives in Statewave's Postgres, not bound to LangGraph's internal state model.

When Statewave (alongside LangGraph) is the right call
comparisons/langchain-memory.md
Statewave vs LangChain / LangGraph Memory › When Statewave (alongside LangGraph) is the right call
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/langchain-memory.md#conceptual-mapping
| LangGraph concept | Statewave concept |
|---|---|
| `thread_id` (per-conversation) | `metadata.session_id` on a Statewave episode (carried for context, not for splitting subjects) |
| `Store` namespace `(user_id, "memories")` | `subject_id = "user-<id>"` |
| `store.put(namespace, key, value)` | `POST /v1/episodes` (raw fact as an event) followed by automatic compilation, OR `POST /v1/memories/compile` |
| `store.search(namespace, query=, limit=N)` | `POST /v1/context` (ranked, token-bounded) |
| Checkpointer-managed graph state | Stays in LangGraph; not a Statewave concern |
Conceptual mapping
comparisons/langchain-memory.md
Statewave vs LangChain / LangGraph Memory › Migration recipe — adding Statewave to a LangGraph agent › Conceptual mapping
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/langchain-memory.md#code-sketch-node-functions
```python
from langgraph.graph import StateGraph
from statewave import StatewaveClient

sw = StatewaveClient("https://your-statewave.example.com", api_key=...)

def remember_node(state):
    """Persist what the user just said to Statewave."""
    last_user_msg = state["messages"][-1].content
    sw.add_episode(
        subject_id=f"user-{state['user_id']}",
        source="chat",
        type="conversation",
        payload={"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": last_user_msg}]},
        metadata={"session_id": state["thread_id"]},
    )
    return state

def recall_node(state):
    """Pull relevant cross-session context for the current task."""
    bundle = sw.get_context(
        subject_id=f"user-{state['user_id']}",
        task=state["current_task"],
        max_tokens=600,
    )
    return {**state, "memory_context": bundle.assembled_context}

# Wire them into your graph however you'd normally compose nodes:
graph = StateGraph(State)
graph.add_node("recall", recall_node)
graph.add_node("agent", agent_node)  # your existing agent node
graph.add_node("remember", remember_node)
graph.add_edge("recall", "agent")
graph.add_edge("agent", "remember")
```

The LangGraph checkpointer continues to handle per-thread graph state. Statewave handles long-term subject memory. They don't conflict.
Code sketch — node functions
comparisons/langchain-memory.md
Statewave vs LangChain / LangGraph Memory › Migration recipe — adding Statewave to a LangGraph agent › Code sketch — node functions
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/langchain-memory.md#side-by-side
| | **LangGraph persistence** | **Statewave** |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary scope** | Per-thread state + cross-thread KV | Cross-session typed memory |
| **Thread state** | Yes — `Checkpointer` saves full graph state per step | Out of scope — Statewave has no graph; thread/session is metadata on episodes |
| **Cross-thread memory** | Yes — `Store` interface, namespaced KV with optional embedding search | Yes — subjects with episodes + compiled memories + ranked retrieval |
| **Ranking** | Not built-in — `store.search()` returns results, you order/score them | First-class: kind priority × recency × task relevance × temporal validity × semantic similarity |
| **Token budgeting** | Not built-in — you slice results to fit your prompt | First-class: `max_tokens` parameter on every context request |
| **Provenance** | Not built-in — implement in node functions | First-class: `source_episode_ids` per memory, immutable episode chain |
| **Determinism** | Not advertised; depends on your retrieval logic + embedding state | Contracted — same inputs return same bundle |
| **Storage** | InMemory / SQLite / Postgres / Cosmos via swappable backends | Postgres + pgvector |
| **Orchestrates the agent loop?** | Yes — that's its primary job | No |
| **Best at** | Being a flexible substrate for agent state | Being a focused runtime for cross-session memory |

The two products **compose** rather than compete head-to-head. LangGraph for graph state and orchestration; Statewave for cross-session, ranked, typed memory.

Side-by-side
comparisons/langchain-memory.md
Statewave vs LangChain / LangGraph Memory › Side-by-side
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/langchain-memory.md#statewave-vs-langchain-langgraph-memory
> **TL;DR** — LangChain's modern memory story is **LangGraph persistence**: per-thread `checkpointer`s persist graph state, and a separate `Store` interface holds key-value memory across threads with optional semantic search via embeddings. Both are deliberately low-level primitives — ranking, scoring, token-budgeting, and provenance are left to the developer. Statewave is the opposite tradeoff: a higher-level memory runtime that ships those decisions baked in, deterministically. Pick LangGraph if your agent loop is already there and you want primitives you control end-to-end; pick Statewave if you want ranked, provenance-traced context bundles without writing them yourself.

Statewave vs LangChain / LangGraph Memory
comparisons/langchain-memory.md
Statewave vs LangChain / LangGraph Memory
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/langchain-memory.md#per-thread-state-checkpointer
> *"When you compile a graph with a checkpointer, a snapshot of the graph state is saved at every step of execution, organised into threads."*

Each checkpoint captures full state channel values, pending node writes, source/step metadata, and a `parent_config` pointer to the prior checkpoint. A `thread_id` is mandatory for persistence and resumption.

Implementations:

- `InMemorySaver` (development)
- `SqliteSaver` / `AsyncSqliteSaver` (via `langgraph-checkpoint-sqlite`)
- `PostgresSaver` / `AsyncPostgresSaver` (production)
- `CosmosDBSaver` (Azure)
Per-thread state — `Checkpointer`
comparisons/langchain-memory.md
Statewave vs LangChain / LangGraph Memory › What LangGraph persistence is › Per-thread state — `Checkpointer`
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/langchain-memory.md#cross-thread-memory-store
> *"What if we want to retain some information across threads?"*

The `Store` interface is namespaced by tuple (e.g. `(user_id, "memories")`):

- `store.put(namespace, key, value)` — write
- `store.search(namespace, query=...)` — read, with optional **"semantic search using embeddings (requires `index` configuration with embedding provider)."**

You configure which fields get embedded via the `fields` parameter, pass a `limit` on search, and manage the lifecycle yourself.
Cross-thread memory — `Store`
comparisons/langchain-memory.md
Statewave vs LangChain / LangGraph Memory › What LangGraph persistence is › Cross-thread memory — `Store`
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/langchain-memory.md#what-langgraph-deliberately-doesnt-do
> *"You can control which parts of your memories get embedded by configuring the `fields` parameter."*

Per LangGraph's docs:

- **No automatic ranking / scoring pipeline.** Retrieval is `store.search()` — you pick `limit`, you compose results, you handle relevance trade-offs.
- **No token-budgeting.** What fits in your prompt is your responsibility.
- **Limited automatic provenance.** Each memory `Item` has `created_at`, `updated_at`, `key`, `namespace`. There's **no automatic lineage tracking** of which conversation turn or LLM reasoning produced the memory; the docs are explicit that *"users must implement custom provenance logic in node functions."*

This is a deliberate design — LangGraph wants to be a substrate, not an opinionated runtime. The trade-off is that "memory that works well across multi-session usage" is a thing you build, not a thing you import.
What LangGraph deliberately doesn't do
comparisons/langchain-memory.md
Statewave vs LangChain / LangGraph Memory › What LangGraph persistence is › What LangGraph deliberately doesn't do
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/langchain-memory.md#what-statewave-is
Statewave is a memory runtime with the opinions baked in:

- **Ranking is a first-class output.** Every memory in a context bundle has been scored against the task and packed against a `max_tokens` budget. Scoring formula is documented in [architecture/ranking.md](../architecture/ranking.md).
- **Provenance is not optional.** Every memory carries `source_episode_ids`. Every episode carries timestamps and metadata. Citation chains are queryable.
- **Determinism is the contract.** Same `(subject_id, task, max_tokens)` returns the same bundle. That's what makes retrieval testable.
- **Compilation is explicit.** Episodes (raw events) and memories (compiled facts) are different things; `/v1/memories/compile` is the boundary. No magic auto-extraction in `put()`.

LangGraph: **substrate**. Statewave: **runtime that opinionated-up the substrate.**

The two compose well. A LangGraph agent can call Statewave for cross-session memory while keeping its checkpointer for in-graph state.

https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/mem0.md#statewave-vs-mem0
> **TL;DR** — Mem0 is a managed memory product with both a hosted SaaS ("Mem0 Platform") and an open-source self-hosted option, focused on personalization for AI assistants. Statewave is a self-hosted-only structured memory runtime focused on deterministic, provenance-traced context for engineering teams that need eval-driven memory. Pick Mem0 if you want a hosted stack with SOC 2 controls and minimal ops; pick Statewave if your data must stay in your infrastructure and you want explainable, reproducible retrieval.

Statewave vs Mem0
comparisons/mem0.md
Statewave vs Mem0
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/mem0.md#what-mem0-is
Per Mem0's docs ([mem0.ai/overview](https://docs.mem0.ai/overview)), Mem0 is positioned as **"the memory engine for AI"** — a managed service that runs "the vector store, graph services, and rerankers" so you don't have to. It exposes **"add, search, update, and delete workflows"** and persists memory across users, agents, and sessions to "cut prompt bloat and repeat questions."

Two product surfaces:

- **Mem0 Platform** — fully managed SaaS. Mem0 advertises **"SOC 2, audit logs, and workspace governance ship by default."**
- **Mem0 OSS** — the open-source library you can self-host.

Internally, Mem0 combines vector search, a graph layer, and a reranker; the docs don't break down which is primary or how the layers compose. Memory types are referenced as "user, agent, and session memory" but the public overview doesn't formalise an episodic / semantic / procedural taxonomy.
What Mem0 is
comparisons/mem0.md
Statewave vs Mem0 › What Mem0 is
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/mem0.md#what-statewave-is
Statewave is a **memory runtime** — a single self-hosted service that records raw events as immutable episodes, compiles them into typed memories (`profile_fact`, `episode_summary`, `procedure`, `artifact_ref`) with confidence scores and validity windows, and assembles ranked, token-bounded context bundles with provenance back to source episodes. Postgres + pgvector is the only storage dependency. No managed plane.

The retrieval contract is deliberately deterministic: same `(subject_id, task, max_tokens)` returns the same bundle. That property is what makes Statewave evaluable — you can write a regression test that retrieval still returns the right facts after a code change. See [architecture/ranking.md](../architecture/ranking.md).

What Statewave is
comparisons/mem0.md
Statewave vs Mem0 › What Statewave is
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/mem0.md#side-by-side
| | **Mem0** | **Statewave** |
|---|---|---|
| **Deployment** | Hosted SaaS (Mem0 Platform) + self-hosted (Mem0 OSS) | Self-hosted only. Postgres + your container platform. |
| **Retrieval model** | Vector + graph + reranker stack (managed under the hood). Composition not exposed in public docs. | Typed-memory ranking: kind priority × recency × task relevance × temporal validity × semantic similarity. Formula and signals documented per memory in the response. |
| **Determinism** | Not advertised. Reranker behaviour is part of the managed stack. | Deterministic by design — same inputs return the same context bundle. Documented in [why-statewave.md](../why-statewave.md). |
| **Memory types** | "User / agent / session memory" (per the public overview). | `profile_fact`, `episode_summary`, `procedure`, `artifact_ref` enums in the response model. |
| **Provenance** | Not surfaced in the public overview. | Every memory returns `source_episode_ids`. Every episode is immutable and timestamped. Citation chain is queryable. |
| **Token-bounded context** | Not advertised as a contract. | First-class: `max_tokens` is part of the context request; bundle reports `token_estimate` against budget. |
| **Compliance** | "SOC 2, audit logs, workspace governance" advertised on the platform tier. | Customer-side — you self-host, you own the controls. No managed plane to audit. |
| **Pricing** | Tiered platform plans + free OSS. Refer to [mem0.ai pricing](https://mem0.ai/pricing). | OSS (AGPLv3) free; commercial license for proprietary use. See [LICENSING.md](https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave/blob/main/LICENSING.md). |
| **API surface** | `add`, `search`, `update`, `delete` per Mem0's overview. | `POST /v1/episodes`, `POST /v1/memories/compile`, `POST /v1/context`, `GET /v1/timeline`, `DELETE /v1/subjects/{id}`. See [api/v1-contract.md](../api/v1-contract.md). |
| **Compilation** | Implicit — the platform manages extraction. | Explicit `/v1/memories/compile` step; pluggable heuristic or LLM compiler. See [architecture/compiler-modes.md](../architecture/compiler-modes.md). |

Statewave vs Mem0 › Side-by-side
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/mem0.md#when-mem0-is-the-right-call
- You want a **managed plane** and don't want to operate Postgres + a runtime yourself.
- You need **SOC 2-ready memory** out of the box and would otherwise have to layer compliance controls onto your own stack.

When Mem0 is the right call
comparisons/mem0.md
Statewave vs Mem0 › When Mem0 is the right call
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/mem0.md#when-statewave-is-the-right-call
- **Self-hosted is non-negotiable.** Your data residency, vendor restriction, or compliance posture rules out a managed plane. Statewave runs entirely inside your infrastructure (heuristic compilation has zero data egress; LLM/embedding choices control the rest — see [privacy-and-data-flow.md](../architecture/privacy-and-data-flow.md)).
- **You want eval-driven memory.** Deterministic retrieval means you can regression-test context quality. Statewave ships a [docs-grounded eval](https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-examples/tree/main/eval-docs-support) and [support-agent benchmark](https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-examples/tree/main/benchmark-support-agent) as runnable examples; the same harness pattern works for your own subject schemes.
- **Provenance is load-bearing.** When a customer asks "why did the agent say X about my account?", you need to trace back to the conversation that produced the fact. Statewave's `source_episode_ids` chain makes that a query, not a forensic exercise.
- **Token-bounded retrieval is part of the contract.** When prompt budget matters (cost, latency, context-window pressure), Statewave packs by ranked score against an explicit `max_tokens` instead of leaving truncation to the caller.

Statewave vs Mem0 › When Statewave is the right call
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/mem0.md#migration-recipe
Mem0's primary primitives are *user memories* indexed by `user_id` plus messages. Statewave's primary primitive is the *subject* — an entity any episode can be written to.
Migration recipe
comparisons/mem0.md
Statewave vs Mem0 › Migration recipe
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/mem0.md#conceptual-mapping
| Mem0 concept | Statewave concept |
|---|---|
| `user_id` (Mem0 user-scoped memory) | `subject_id` (Statewave subject — typically `user-<id>`, see [subject-design.md](../subject-design.md)) |
| `agent_id` | A separate subject per agent if you want shared agent memory across users; otherwise carry as episode metadata. |
| `session_id` | `metadata.session_id` on the episode. Statewave does not split sessions into separate subjects — sessions are spans inside a subject. |
| `add(messages, user_id=...)` | `POST /v1/episodes` with `subject_id`, `payload.messages`, `source="chat"`, `type="conversation"` |
| `search(query, user_id=...)` | `POST /v1/context` with `subject_id`, `task=query`, `max_tokens` |
| Mem0-extracted facts | Statewave-compiled memories — call `/v1/memories/compile` after batch ingest, or rely on automatic compile after each episode |
| Memory metadata | Statewave `metadata_` JSONB on each row (free-form) |
Conceptual mapping
comparisons/mem0.md
Statewave vs Mem0 › Migration recipe › Conceptual mapping
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/mem0.md#code-sketch-python-sdk
```python
# Before — Mem0
from mem0 import Memory
m = Memory()
m.add([{"role": "user", "content": "I prefer Python with type hints"}], user_id="alice")
results = m.search("what does alice prefer?", user_id="alice")

# After — Statewave
from statewave import StatewaveClient
sw = StatewaveClient("https://your-statewave.example.com", api_key=...)

# Ingest as an episode
sw.add_episode(
    subject_id="user-alice",
    source="chat",
    type="conversation",
    payload={"messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "I prefer Python with type hints"}]},
)

# Retrieve as a context bundle
bundle = sw.get_context(
    subject_id="user-alice",
    task="what does alice prefer?",
    max_tokens=600,
)
# bundle.assembled_context  → drop into your prompt
# bundle.facts              → list of compiled memories
# bundle.provenance         → fact_ids + episode_ids cited
```
Code sketch (Python SDK)
comparisons/mem0.md
Statewave vs Mem0 › Migration recipe › Code sketch (Python SDK)
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/mem0.md#bulk-migration
If you already have a Mem0 corpus to import:

1. Export Mem0 memories per user (refer to Mem0's export/dump tooling).
2. For each Mem0 memory, write a Statewave episode with the original text in the payload and Mem0's metadata in the episode's `metadata_` field. Carry the original Mem0 memory id in `provenance.original_id` so you can reverse-trace if needed.
3. Run `POST /v1/memories/compile` once per migrated subject. Compilation is idempotent.
4. Spot-check retrieval on representative queries before flipping the read path.

Bulk migration
comparisons/mem0.md
Statewave vs Mem0 › Migration recipe › Bulk migration
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/mem0.md#honest-gaps
Statewave does not match Mem0 on every axis. If any of these are blockers, factor them in:

- **No hosted SaaS.** You run it. Cost of ops is yours.
- **No managed compliance plane.** SOC 2 / HIPAA / etc. are your responsibility. Statewave gives you the technical controls (delete-by-subject, no required outbound calls in heuristic mode); you supply the audit programme.
- **No graph-of-entities first-class concept.** Statewave is typed-memory, not a knowledge graph. If you need relational reasoning across entities (e.g. "find every account Sarah is connected to"), you'd build that on top.
- **Single-node only today.** Horizontal scaling is roadmap, not shipped. Statewave is honest about this — see the *Current limitations* section in [why-statewave.md](../why-statewave.md).

> **TL;DR** — Letta (the company that grew out of the [MemGPT](https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.08560) research project) is a stateful-agent runtime: an LLM-as-OS architecture where the model manages its own memory, context, and reasoning loop, exposed via a hosted platform and a desktop "Letta Code" app. Statewave is a memory layer that any agent stack can call — it does not run the agent loop. Pick Letta if you want a complete agent runtime out of the box; pick Statewave if you've already built (or chosen) your agent orchestration and just need durable, ranked context that survives sessions.

Statewave vs Letta
comparisons/letta.md
Statewave vs Letta
https://github.com/smaramwbc/statewave-docs/blob/main/comparisons/letta.md#honest-gaps
- **Statewave doesn't run agents.** If you want one product that does memory + tools + loops, that's not us. Letta does this; we don't.
- **No MemFS-equivalent for personal-use desktop agents.** Letta Code's git-backed local memory is a different product shape. Statewave is server-side.
- **No skills system.** Letta has a skills concept for reusable agent capabilities. That's an agent-runtime feature; Statewave is a memory layer.
- **Different mental model.** Letta operators think in *agents*; Statewave operators think in *subjects*. Teams already fluent in MemGPT-style memory management have non-trivial cognitive cost when switching.
